author: @mikelastname
text: Systems thinking is also super helpful when debugging something that isn't working. By breaking up a complex design into logical system components (functional blocks) you can then assess if each block is performing the function required if its inputs and outputs are nominal. Once you find the block not performing, you can dig into that functional capability and find defective components (or even sub systems) and this way you quickly find faults in highly complex systems.

This is the most important thing I have ever learned and I appreciate your similar approach to learning and teaching. Keep calm and carry on from Canada mate!

author: @RogerHyam
text: Wisdom is knowing that the way you have structured your knowledge is contingent on the functions that you have been trying to enable and isn't some absolute truth. An example from my world: I do scientific database and web stuff. The functions I support are curating largish datasets and sharing them on line. I work with people who write software to do analyses and produce scientific papers. We overlaps in many of the same pieces of information but our world views are different. They think they can knock up a web service but it doesn't really work out. I think I can do an analysis but set about it in a really inefficient way. Perhaps geniuses are those people who can not only see the different arrangements of information in different peoples filing cabinets they can also re-arrange their own filing cabinet really quickly! Non attachment to views.

author: @iExistOne
text:
Designing a Solar Collection and Storage System:

This is an intricate system with several variables that must be considered. Ordered accordingly.

4. Panels:
How much wattage are you going to generate?
What is your total wattage consumption currently or expected?
How much panel wattage do you need?
This is a sizing question whose variables are, sunshine and what it's going to power.
How much voltage per panel?
Necessary for series wiring, voltage is additive and the Charge Controller is limited to a maximum Voltage input.
How much amperage per panel?
Necessary for parallel wiring, amperage is additive and the Charge Controller has a maximum amperage input.
How much total watts per panel?

3. Charge Controller:
How many panels are you going to connect to the Charge Controller?
What type of batteries are you going to connect to the Charge Controller?
Charge Controllers are rated by the max output amperage. A 60A Charge Controller can charge up to 60A to the batteries. At 60A the Charge Controllers input is measured in max combined voltage. Typically, the higher the Amp rating of the Charge Controller means you can connect a greater total number of panels.

2. Batteries:
What type of batteries are you going to use in your solar installation?
Different battery technologies have different Depth of Discharge capacities. LAB, FLA vehicle batteries usually 25% DOD, Trojan Solar batteries 80%, LiFePo DOD is nearly 100%.
Depth of Discharge is the usable quantity of energy you have available. A batteries life cycle rating is at the maximum discharge capacity.

1. Sunshine:
What is the average hours of Sun per day at your location?
When is the least amount of hours of sunshine?
When is the largest amount of hours of sunshine?
How many hours do you need in order to power your equipment?
This is necessary to determine the size of your battery storage system.

For example, all cars have an alternator and they all work the same way. How they’re fitted is the thing that changes slightly from manufacturer to manufacturer, understanding the principles allows him to now replace an alternator on pretty much any vehicle.

author: @timmyssweet
text:
Yes Robert, energy is a great analogy.  The input could be solar, wind or hydro.  If you live beside a reliable river, hydro might be the best input.  If you live in Australia, solar might be your best input.  The input is critical.  Some people because still buy windmills and then complain about the lack of generation.

You highlighted the controller decisions that I was not aware of.  Enphase inverters can also supply a little bit of power when the grid goes down - without a battery - for running lights and other light load, which might be important for some people - with a large array and no battery.

The biggest challenge that I see with solar installation is sizing the system.  You have skirted around that issue by going cheap.  For example, the solar needs to match the inverter and the inverter needs to match the battery and load.  Micro-inverter also mitigate that issue, which you sort of addressed.  

At end of the day, ask your installer, give them your preference and go with their recommendations.  A registered system, needs maintenance and compliance - unless you are installing a small / low voltage system, which I think is under 48 volt DC in Australia, and a DIYer, in my personal opinion.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I absolutely agree with you mate and thank you for pointing the Inverter issue

replies.author: @offgridwanabe
replies.text: Enphase micro inverters require both AC and DC to produce more AC power as long as you make both they work.

author: @patrick_worldoflies
text:
Totally agree with your point here. Learning needs time and practice.
Btw: I tried to look through your videos (there maybe are a bit many) because I want to try to build a small wind generator. Feels like being very late to a party ^^
I wanted to start with an axial flux generator, but I wanted to ask about the wire first. In all the videos I have seen you make spools and windings,
you talk about "wire". Everything I have read otherwise says it has to be "magnet wire", but in some of your vids it seems like you use uncoated wire. 
For example, in vid 1800 you just twisted uncoated wire? But wouldn't that shortcircuit it? Is there only a need for coated wire if it's a motor? 
Am I missing something fundamental? Thought I'd ask before buying magnet wire because of budget reasons.

replies.author: @chrismalcomson2824
replies.text: The wire in video 1800 for the serpentine is enameled copper wire. The same type used in electric motors, generators, etc. So it is actually coated. To wind a coil with bare wire would be a huge mistake because they can (and will) short each other out. You would basically end up with a big, thick coil with one turn. I hope that makes some sense and helps. 🙂

replies.author: @patrick_worldoflies
replies.text: ​@chrismalcomson2824yeah, thanks! sometimes it's weird at first when you're not familiar with the common terminology 😅

replies.author: @chrismalcomson2824
replies.text: @patrick_worldoflies Glad I was able to help..I understand some of it is very confusing for beginners. I used to rewind electric motors, generators, transformers, and welders, so when it comes to winding stuff, it all makes sense to me. 🙂

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: sorry mate mu bad - i just took it as read that it would be understood that in that situation it is always enamel coated wire - as you said otherwise it would just short - I should have been more clear - my apologies

replies.author: @patrick_worldoflies
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering No, that's totally fine and normal! No need to apologise for that. We all get into that rut of assuming some form of common knowledge. I'm like that with computers and some other stuff, too. It's an efficiency thing imo, but we fall into the trap that our topic is harder to access for 'outsiders'.
I just think that maybe as a culture we should be more aware of that tendency, but that is in no way direct critique!

author: @WarblesWarkly
text:
lol I see someone isn't happy with your choices on this project 😂😂😂

Mr Smith, first and foremost, you're awesome.

Second, just do the project as you're going to do it. Many of us understand that you do projects in which they are as accessible and as affordable to someone who may want to replicate it.

Examples off the top of my head are the prussian blue and tea developed pictures (so awesome I copied it for my daughters 1st grade history project, which by the way, the teacher was convinced it was an actual old photo, i can give a bit more details of you want) and another example are the sand (battery) heaters you made, what, 3 years ago?

I still use my v2.1.2 heater. I opted to use sterno fuel that I make, but only because I can make the fuel with my kids.

All the simple household stuff like that is what I teach my kids.

Extra batteries, or making a battery in a dire situation. Or making fire/heat in a dire situation. Finding food. Finding water.

How to properly clean and sterilize tools for food, hunting, and when they're older, healing. (I can do a basic suture).

The fact that you inspire critical thinking, curious thinking, analytical thinking... damn bruv. You are at the top of the list!

(Though to be fair you're tied in the top spot because it's too difficult to say who's actually number 1, but I guarantee you'll never fall out of the top 3 in my mind.)

That's a lot a rambling, my apologies, just wanted to try and explain that even though i get excited and want to see you do projects in a particular way, it doesn't really matter so long as I get to watch the conclusion of the project!

Stay awesome, Mr Smith!

author: @offgridwanabe
text: Oh ya the buffet has a lot of choices and the size of your plate determines what you can put on it. No sense in making a 48 volt system if you only have 3 12 volt batteries, or a pwn charger that only does 12 or 24 volts or the wire you have is not big enough to carry a low voltage load. Resistance  is futile the solar world will determine the outcome lol cheers mate.

author: @Brown_Potato
text: Such a simple topic but well said. Honestly at a certain level this is totally fine, but once you add dishonesty into the mix it becomes tougher to draw the lines. In the scientific field choosing a correct manufacturer for a signal multiplexor can be a life or death situation if you get the data you want, or how clean the signals turn out to me. Furthermore the waters are muddied by finance administrators who refuse to bother reading into the data sheets or reputation of some suppliers....
